comments.author: @skiandsea-m
comments.text: Svalbard is a magnificent place and this video has some good footage from the islands. However, several times throughout this video you are not showing films clips from Svalbard at all, but from Norway mainland and probably from one or two other countries as well. Film clips with woods in them are clearly not from Svalbard, e.g. most of those of the Northern lights, which are clearly from Norway mainland. There are other such film clips too. This reduces the credibility and quality of documentaries from this producer/publisher. It is not ok to publish 'documentaries' like this one, with inserted film clips throughout, which are clearly not from the destination you announce. (The polar bear clips are highly likely from Svalbard, just to mention that.) The ending: The ending-comments about anthropogenic causes of climate change should also be left out. The publisher may not be aware of this, but there is no real scientific evidense (no experimental evidence) of the claims that anthropogenic emissions of CO2 cause climate change/variations in/rise in average temperature on Earth. Introducing this idea at the ending reduces the quality of the video, which otherwise has some good points in it. (If one aspect of this video was/is to throw some light on wnvironmental protection, then the following sad phenomenon - which also sadly reaches Svalbard mainly via large ocean currents - is what should be focused: The plastic and nano-microplastic pollution of seas (but also, as Norwegian researchers has found, in the atmosphere even as far north as Svalbard.) •• That, dear publisher is based on solid scientific knowledge (unlike the faulty ideas of anthropogenic emissions of CO2s influence on earth's climate). That, if anything, is what should be focused in a video like this when dealing with serious environmental problems. Also for all other living organisms.

replies.author: @skiandsea-m
replies.text: @trueglobe66 Thank you.

replies.author: @trueglobe66
replies.text: I appreciate your detailed feedback regarding the footage sources and will certainly keep your points in mind for my future documentary projects. ✨

comments.text: First time on Svalbard was 38 years ago (1988). Was in Belsund , Longierbyen Barentsburg and on the island of Prince Karl Forland... I have visited about 50 countries in my whole life, but Svalbard has become the most firmly established in my soul and heart..

replies.author: @trueglobe66
replies.text: What a beautiful memory. Visiting Svalbard back in 1988 must have been a completely different experience from today. I’m curious — what made Svalbard stay so deeply in your heart after visiting so many countries: the silence, the isolation, the Arctic landscape, or the people you met there?
